headline_missing_perspectives: Wan Azizah Wan Ismail as first woman in Malaysian history to found and lead a political party (Parti Keadilan Nasional, 4 April 1999); the dossier reduces this to a single timeline entry without naming the historic gender milestone.
Orang Asli political agency — Parti Orang Asli Malaysia (Asli) was officially registered in December 2022 and plans to contest 11 seats in GE16.
Opposition suppression in Sarawak — GPS denies development funds to opposition-held constituencies; only 2 of 82 Sarawak state seats are held by the opposition (as of mid-2025).

2014 Sabah RCI on illegal immigrants — Project IC documentation of systematic citizenship grants to undocumented migrants and their voter-roll inclusion; none of the RCI's recommendations implemented by 2021.
JAKIM and the religious bureaucracy as a structural condition enabling Islamist party formation; no constitutional basis but substantial political delegation since 1997.
Class disaggregation within ethnic categories (B40 / M40 / T20 Malay; SME vs corporate Chinese; plantation vs professional Indian) is treated as ethnic monolith in the cleavage section.
Comparative populism literature (Mudde, Müller, Levitsky & Way) is not engaged despite strong parallels to global populist launches.

UNDI18 and automatic voter registration added approximately 5.8 million new voters in 2022 (~30% of electorate) — the dossier treats the electorate as a stable pool rather than one undergoing generational transformation.
Bajau community in Sabah (17.3% of state population, Warisan base) and KDM/Sino-Kadazan identities not named in the Sabah-volatility analysis.

international_parallels.relevance: Post-1989 fragmentation from Congress dominance to coalition era — 113 contesting parties by 1989
Personalist party system as extreme endpoint of launch-and-pivot model
Effective number of parliamentary parties (ENPP) 9.91 — fragmentation without electoral system change
LDP factions as quasi-parties — alternative to forming new parties
Inverse case — PAP dominance under FPTP+GRC system suppresses new-party launches entirely
ANC's 2024 vote share fell below 50% (40.2%) — accelerating fragmentation similar to UMNO post-2018
PRI fragmented after losing presidency in 2000 — closest international parallel to UMNO post-2018

alternative_framings: Bersatu success may be more about Mahathir's personal capital than the three structural ingredients (evidence: Bersatu collapsed from 13 seats to ~1 after Mahathir's 2020 resignation).
What appears as ethnic cleavage may be intra-elite contestation, with ethnic mobilisation as an instrument rather than a reflection of mass polarisation.
GPS stability is not merely fiscal — it includes active suppression of competition (only 2 of 82 state seats opposition-held).
missing_figures: Campaign finance money-flow diagram (established parties vs new parties)
GE15 results map by parliamentary constituency
Women MPs in Malaysia over time (1959–2022) line chart
Class breakdown of voting behaviour bar chart
International comparison timeline (party launches and fragmentation events across countries)
gender_gaps: Timeline does not identify women-led launches.
Women's wings (Wanita UMNO, Wanita MCA, Wanita PKR, Muslimat PAS) as parallel structures not mentioned.
Voter-perspective conclusion is gender-neutral; treats half the electorate as politically homogeneous with men.
economic_gaps: Ethnic categories treated as monolith; no class disaggregation.
Patronage is ethnically structured — Indian-majority parties have weakest capital base; no successful Indian-majority launch since MIC.
Sarawak fiscal lever should be analysed as resource curse, not neutral structural advantage.
geographic_gaps: East Malaysia exoticised as 'laboratory' rather than centred as integral to Malaysian politics.
State-level base rates (Kelantan/Penang/Johor) not disaggregated from federal base rates.
MA63 mentioned but not explained — assumes background knowledge.
overall_assessment: Dossier D001 is a substantial and analytically rigorous work that successfully applies comparative political theory to Malaysian party formation. Its core framework is coherent and well-evidenced. However, the dossier exhibits significant blind spots: it systematically underrepresents women's agency in party formation; neglects indigenous communities beyond the Malay-Bumiputera binary; treats Sarawak's GPS dominance as purely fiscal while ignoring active suppression of competition; treats ethnicity as the master cleavage without adequately disaggregating by income class; and does not engage with the international populism literature. The dossier would benefit from a companion piece addressing these gaps, or from integrating gender, class, and indigenous perspectives into a revised edition.
